narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, your law school application stood out in some ways, like your choice to pursue full-time studies, which aligns with the vast majority of students in the dataset at 92.7%. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' academic records and bar exam outcomes, predicted a 52% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try—tipping just over the threshold that led to your rejection. This prediction hinges on how your profile stacks up against thousands of similar students, focusing on key indicators of bar success like early and later academic performance and standardized test scores. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2nd, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5.53 average and signaled weaker performance relative to peers at that stage. Next in importance, your LSAT score of 35.5—slightly below the 36.7 average—worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward bar passage by showing competitive testing ability. Rounding out the top influences, your Year 1 decile of 1st, while impressive and above the 5.71 average, wasn't strong enough to fully offset the later dip, still nudging the model toward concern. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.8, below the 3.21 norm, played a supporting role but wasn't among the heaviest hitters here. Overall, the model saw your trajectory as riskier than most applicants who passed the bar, with that Year 3 performance standing out as the biggest red flag compared to typical successful students. To turn this around, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further or strengthening your recent academics through additional coursework or a post-bac program before reapplying. Many students in your position have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps, so targeting that later decile could make a real difference next time. You're clearly capable, especially with your strong start, and with focused improvements, law school doors can reopen.
